If you hold it, keep your thumbs way back so there's room for the baby's lower jaw to take a big bite, and keep fingers well away from where his lower jaw and chin need to be.

instruction: Is it okay to put my finger under my baby's nose to help his jaw while nursing?
output: You can rest your index finger under your own nose to feel how the lower jaw moves, but when nursing do not put fingers where the baby's lower jaw needs room. Keep your fingers totally away from the area where his chin and lower jaw must be.

instruction: Do I have to lift or hold my breast the whole time during a feeding?
output: Not necessarily. First try starting where gravity has placed your breast. If you do lift the breast for the baby, you will probably have to hold it throughout the feeding. Some babies do need the breast stabilized or lifted slightly to make breathing easier, but unless you are very long-breasted or wide-breasted it will probably be easier to let the breast be where it naturally sits.

instruction: My baby's nose looks buried in my breast and I'm worried he can't breathe — what can I try?
output: It’s reasonable to be worried. In most cases babies get plenty of air if their heads are tilted back so the nose is free or nearly free. If his nose is buried, try shifting him to tip his head back more, maybe by pulling his back and shoulders in closer. You can also lift the breast slightly by pressing the flat of your hand on your chest between breast and collarbone and lifting.

instruction: Does the baby's upper jaw have to be over the nipple for good nursing?
output: No. The baby's lower jaw is the moving jaw and needs plenty of room; the upper jaw isn't critical for nursing.

instruction: Should I try to latch my baby when his mouth is wide open on purpose?
output: Mothers used to be told to latch when the mouth was wide open, but mammal mothers don’t take that much control. Unless there's a specific need, the baby will probably do best if he picks the moment. Your job is to make that moment possible by having a well-supported baby in easy reach of the breast.

instruction: Does tipping my baby's head back help with swallowing while nursing?
output: Yes. Tipping his head back a bit helps swallowing; it's easier to swallow when the chin is lifted. The baby does better if his chin is in firm contact with the breast and his nose is lifted free or nearly free of the breast.

instruction: If I lift my breast to make breathing easier, will I need to hold it the whole feeding?
output: If you lift the breast for your baby, you'll probably have to hold it throughout the feeding. Some babies do need the breast stabilized or lifted slightly to make breathing easier, but it's not necessary for every feeding.

instruction: My baby keeps waving his arms and can't seem to latch — what might help?
output: Babies who don't feel stable may wave their arms while they move their head to search, which can make latching difficult. Holding him with his shoulders tucked in closely to you and supporting him behind his back and shoulders helps keep him stable while he moves his head to search.

instruction: Where should my thumbs be if I hold my breast like a sandwich for nursing?
output: When holding the breast like a sandwich, keep your thumbs way back on the breast so there is room for a big lower-jaw bite, and keep fingers away from the lower-jaw area.

instruction: Do specific holds or positions always matter for breastfeeding success?
output: When all is going well, specific holds and positions don't really matter because you and your baby figure out your own system. However, holds and positions can help mothers and babies get a good latch when trouble occurs, for example after labor medications.

instruction: Is it okay if my baby's nose is just lightly touching my breast while he nurses?
output: Yes. Even if his nose is lightly touching the breast, most babies can still breathe just fine. If he truly couldn't breathe, he lets go.

instruction: Do I have to lift or hold my breast throughout the feeding?
output: Not necessarily. First try starting where gravity has already placed your breast. If you lift the breast for the baby, you will probably have to hold it throughout the feeding. Some babies do need the breast stabilized or lifted slightly to make breathing easier. Unless you are very long-breasted or wide-breasted, it will probably be easier to let your breast be where it naturally sits.

instruction: How can I lift my breast slightly if I need to?
output: You can lift your breast slightly by pressing the flat of your hand on your chest between the breast and your collarbone and lifting.

instruction: My baby seems to need the breast stabilized to breathe while nursing — what holds or positions can help?
output: Some babies do need the breast stabilized or lifted slightly to make breathing easier. Positions that can help include the cradle hold, cross-cradle (across-the-chest) hold, the clutch hold, and side-lying; each positions the baby so the chest is snuggled into the breast and lets you support or shape the breast as needed.

instruction: What is the cradle hold and how should I position my baby's head and body?
output: In the cradle hold, hold your baby's front against yours by supporting him behind his back and shoulders. His head should rest on your forearm or wrist (not the crook of your elbow) and his hip on or near your thigh. His chest is snuggled into your breast with your nipple near his nose; as he opens to take the nipple, gently snuggle him closer. If he seems confused, shift him so his cheek rests near your nipple or use a nipple-tilt technique.

instruction: How do I do the across-the-chest or cross-cradle hold?
output: Hold the baby with the arm opposite the breast he will latch onto, with his front under your breasts and his hip near or resting on your thigh. Use your hand to support his neck (not the back of his head) while your arm hugs him; his chest should be snuggled against the mound of your breast. Your other hand can shape or stabilize your breast. As he opens to take the nipple, gently snuggle his chest closer; touching his cheek or using a nipple tilt may help.

instruction: What is the clutch hold and when is it suggested?
output: The clutch hold positions the baby next to you with his front against your side, your hand under his shoulders and neck, and his body supported between your arm and the side of your body. It is often suggested for mothers who have had C-sections because the baby’s weight is kept away from the incision. The baby will be a bit below your breast, which may work well if your breasts are fairly large; you may need to move him into a semisitting position if your breasts are smaller.

instruction: How do I breastfeed in the side-lying position at night?
output: Lay your baby on her side facing you and pull her down so your nipple is level with her nose or eye. You can align your breast to match her mouth, keeping your fingers out of the way and rolling the nipple in last. As she latches, press on the middle of her back to bring her closer; she may be somewhat arched, and bringing her middle back closer helps keep her head tipped back. Tuck a small rolled towel or receiving blanket behind her back to hold her there if you like.

instruction: My baby opens but then seems confused about latching — what can I do to help him take the nipple?
output: If he seems confused as he opens, gently snuggle him closer so his cheek rests near your nipple or use the nipple-tilt technique. You can also touch his cheek to encourage him as he opens to take the nipple.

instruction: Do specific holds and positions matter once breastfeeding is going well?
output: When all is going well, specific holds and positions don’t really matter because you and your baby will figure out your own system. Positions are most useful when you are having trouble getting started, to provide a recipe to follow until you know what works for you.

instruction: Why are certain holds taught or recommended in hospitals?
output: Helpers developed holds and positions to help mothers and babies get a good latch, especially for babies who may be affected by labor medications. The cross-cradle hold, for example, works especially well with small babies and is often taught in hospitals.

instruction: How should I support my baby's neck and head in the cross-cradle hold?
output: In the cross-cradle hold your hand should support the baby’s neck (not the back of his head) while your arm hugs him against you; his chest should be snuggled against your breast.

instruction: If my breasts are fairly large, which position might be easier for nursing?
output: The clutch hold may work well if your breasts are fairly large because the baby will be a bit below the breast in that position.

instruction: What is the clutch hold and why might it be suggested after a C-section?
output: The clutch hold positions your baby next to you, front against your side, with your hand under his shoulders and neck and his body supported between your arm and your side. The baby will be a bit below your breast. It is often suggested after a C-section because the baby’s weight is kept away from the incision.

instruction: My breasts are small. Do I need to change the clutch hold position?
output: You can use the clutch hold, but your baby may be a bit below your breast. If your breasts are smaller, you may need to move him into a semisitting position so he reaches the nipple more easily.

instruction: How do I position my baby for side-lying nursing?
output: Lay your baby on her side facing you and pull her down so your nipple is level with her nose or eye. You can make a sandwich of your breast aligned to her mouth with your fingers well out of the way, rolling the nipple in last. As she latches, press on the middle of her back to bring her closer and help keep her head tipped back. Tucking a small rolled towel or receiving blanket behind her back can hold her there and free a hand.

instruction: Is side-lying useful for night feedings?
output: Yes. Side-lying can be especially helpful at night and can also help a baby who is having difficulty in other positions.

instruction: My baby arches when latching. What can I do to tip his head back?
output: Press on the middle of his back to bring him closer; bringing the middle of his back closer helps keep his head tipped back.

instruction: I had a C-section and can’t get into a semi-reclining position. How can I latch my baby?
output: You can lie flat on your back and let the baby lie on your chest while avoiding your incision, or kneel at your side with someone’s help as he finds the nipple. You can also use a clutch hold or sit upright with a pillow or two behind your back and a pillow on your lap to protect your incision.

instruction: If I used my hand to help my baby latch, can I let go right away?
output: Don’t relax immediately or he could lose your nipple. Give your baby time to get everything arranged in his mouth and start sucking. Then slowly relax the hand on your breast, and if relaxing makes your breast sag away, bring your baby in closer. Rearrange yourself comfortably and settle in.

instruction: Can I nurse while standing up? What should I do?
output: Sometimes babies do better if you stand up. You can sway or bounce gently to help calm your baby. The lack of a lap can be helpful. Keep your baby stable, let him lead the way, and try angling his legs down instead of holding him horizontal.

instruction: My baby seems to be getting milk but nursing hurts me a lot. Is that considered nursing well?
output: Nursing is going well if the baby is able to get the milk he needs without getting too tired AND you both feel comfortable. If you are feeling pain, then that part of the description isn’t working and you should seek help from a lactation consultant or healthcare provider.

instruction: Can I use a small towel to help keep my baby in the side-lying position?
output: Yes. If you keep a small towel or receiving blanket rolled up nearby, you can tuck it behind her back to hold her there and have your hand free to stroke and enjoy her.

instruction: Why might a baby do better without being held in a lap when nursing?
output: Sometimes the lack of a lap helps; for example, standing and nursing removes the lap and can help some babies settle. Swaying or gentle bouncing while standing can also help calm the baby.

instruction: What does "make a sandwich of your breast" mean when helping a baby latch on side-lying?
output: It means aligning your breast to match her mouth with your fingers well out of the way, and rolling the nipple in last so the breast is positioned for her mouth as she latches.

instruction: Will the clutch hold work if my baby is smaller or my breasts are larger?
output: In the clutch hold the baby will be a bit below your breast, which may work well if your breasts are fairly large. If your breasts are smaller you may need to move him into a semisitting position.

instruction: My baby had trouble starting each nursing at first but later could nurse in different positions. Is that normal?
output: Yes. Some mothers find it hard at first but later are able to nurse in different positions and while doing other things as they and the baby become more comfortable.

instruction: How should I support my baby’s body in the clutch hold?
output: Support his body between your arm and the side of your body, and place your hand under his shoulders and neck to stabilize him.

instruction: I'm recovering from a C-section and can't recline—how can I position myself to breastfeed without upsetting my incision?
output: You can lie flat on your back and let the baby lie on your chest while avoiding your incision. With someone's help you can kneel at your side as the baby finds the nipple. You can also use a clutch hold or sit upright with a pillow or two behind your back and a pillow on your lap to protect the incision.

instruction: After I use my hand to get my baby latched, can I let go right away?
output: Don’t relax immediately. Give your baby time to arrange everything in his mouth and start sucking. Once he’s sucking, slowly relax the hand on your breast and bring the baby closer if relaxing makes your breast sag away. Then rearrange yourself comfortably and settle in.

instruction: My latch hurts even though the baby seems attached—what should I do?
output: Pain while nursing or poor milk transfer means something needs adjusting. You can put a finger in the corner of his mouth to break the suction and slip your nipple out, but try not to do that repeatedly. Instead, get help from a lactation consultant or healthcare provider to find a more comfortable approach.

instruction: How do I break suction to take my nipple out of my baby's mouth?
output: Put a finger in the corner of the baby’s mouth, right between the gums, to break the suction. Then slip your nipple out. Try not to do this too many times because it can be frustrating for the baby.

instruction: Can you explain the nipple-tilt technique for getting a deeper latch?
output: Put your thumb or a finger near the base of your nipple on the side where the baby’s upper lip will be. Press or tug a little so the nipple tilts away from the baby. Present that full bit of breast to tease the baby’s lower face. If she opens wide reaching for the nipple, snuggle her straight in without shifting her. Use your thumb or finger to help push or tuck the bit of breast into her mouth; the nipple is the last part in, tilting away and unrolling inside her mouth. Keep the baby snug against your body, her chest against the mound of your breast, and let her control her head.

instruction: Is it safe to put my finger near the baby's jaw while positioning for latching?
output: Keep fingers well away from the baby’s lower jaw. The upper-jaw finger, though, can be right next to the nipple to help present the breast.

instruction: Should I use pillows to support my baby while nursing?
output: If your body supports your baby, there’s no need for pillows. If you need support for your arm, a squishy pillow or two may help. Supporting the baby on a pillow can put extra space between his body and yours and has caused more sore nipples than it has cured. In some situations a flat-surfaced pillow can be a temporary help, especially with a small baby.

instruction: My nipples feel sore when I use a pillow under the baby—could the pillow be the problem?
output: Supporting your baby on a pillow can put extra space between his body and yours and has caused more sore nipples than it has cured. If using a pillow means you have to lift your breast to your baby, you probably don’t need the pillow. A flat-surfaced pillow may work better than a rounded one, especially with a small baby.

instruction: I sit to nurse but my legs are short and my lower back hurts—any simple adjustments?
output: Try a low footstool (or a thick book, diaper bag, or a coffee-table crossbar) to raise your lap. That can help support your arm and the baby and relax your lower back.

instruction: When I relax my hand after a latch, the baby often loses the nipple—what can I do?
output: Don’t relax immediately; give your baby time to arrange his mouth and start sucking. Once he’s sucking, slowly relax your hand, and bring the baby in closer if relaxing makes your breast sag away. If needed, break the suction and try again, but avoid doing that repeatedly, and seek help finding a more comfortable approach.

instruction: How can I help my baby take a deeper mouthful of breast?
output: Narrow the breast "sandwich" by compressing it so it’s easier for the baby to get a deep mouthful. You can combine this with the nipple-tilt technique. Keep your fingers well away from the baby’s lower jaw; the upper-jaw finger can be right next to the nipple.

instruction: Is it okay if my thumb briefly goes under my baby's upper lip when I tuck in the breast?
output: Yes. It’s fine if your thumb or finger ends up under the baby’s upper lip for a moment while tucking in the last bit of breast. Remember to keep the baby snug against your body and let her control her head.

instruction: My newborn is very small and tends to roll into pillow cracks—what kind of pillow should I try?
output: You may do better with a flat-surfaced pillow than with a rounded one, especially with a small baby because they tend to roll into the crack. Keep in mind supporting the baby on a pillow can create extra space and has caused more sore nipples than it cured.

instruction: If using a pillow means I have to lift my breast to my baby, should I keep using it?
output: If using a pillow means you have to lift your breast to your baby, you probably don’t need the pillow.

instruction: How do I know if breastfeeding is going well beyond how it looks?
output: Good breastfeeding is about how it feels and how well it works, not how it looks. Your baby is nursing well if he is able to get the milk he needs without getting too tired and if you both feel comfortable. If you’re feeling pain or the baby isn’t getting milk well, something needs adjusting.

instruction: How can I tilt my nipple to help my baby get more breast when latching?
output: Tilting the nipple can give your baby more breast. You can combine a nipple tilt with narrowing the breast sandwich to help your baby get a deeper mouthful. When you do this, keep the lower-jaw fingers well away from the nipple; the upper-jaw finger can be right next to the nipple.

instruction: What does 'narrowing the sandwich' mean and how does it help with latching?
output: 'Narrowing the sandwich' means compressing a very full breast so it is easier for your baby to take a deep mouthful, similar to compressing a fat sandwich to fit in your mouth. You can combine this with a nipple tilt. Remember to keep the lower-jaw fingers well away from the nipple and the upper-jaw finger next to the nipple.

instruction: Can I use pillows to make nursing more comfortable?
output: If your body already supports your baby, you don’t need pillows. A squishy pillow or two may help if you need support for your arm, but supporting your baby on a pillow can put extra space between you and has caused more sore nipples than it has cured. A flat-surfaced pillow may work better than a rounded one for a small baby. If using a pillow means you have to lift your breast to your baby, you probably don’t need the pillow.

instruction: My nipples have been sore since I started using a nursing pillow — could the pillow be the cause?
output: Supporting a baby on a pillow can put extra space between your body and the baby and has caused more sore nipples than it has cured. A flat-surfaced pillow is less likely to create a crack for a small baby; and if the pillow means you must lift your breast to the baby, you probably don’t need it.

instruction: Should I use a footstool while nursing if my legs are short?
output: If you’re nursing sitting up and have short legs, a low footstool (or a thick book, diaper bag, or coffee-table crossbar) may raise your lap to support your arm and baby and help relax your lower back. You can also try crossing your legs or raising just one foot so your baby’s feet are lower than his head.

instruction: My baby won't latch after an epidural — is that something that happens?
output: Medications such as an epidural or interventions like induction or forceps can make some babies less responsive in the usual way; the effects can last for days or weeks. Other babies may have physical or neurological difficulties. This can be a temporary setback; many mothers have worked through it and the baby may get the hang of nursing with time and help. A full-term, healthy baby does not usually need any food or liquids for at least 24 to 36 hours, so there is time to work on nursing with support.

instruction: If my baby isn't nursing yet, what should I do to keep my milk supply ready?
output: Keep your milk flowing. Your milk supply will start to increase automatically after the first couple of days whether you remove milk or not, but the amount you remove tells your breasts how much you’ll need. If your baby isn’t nursing, start hand-expressing or pumping so your breasts are ready when the baby can nurse. In the first few days colostrum is thick and usually easier to remove by hand expressing; massage and breast compressions help. You can ask for help expressing if needed.

instruction: How long and how often should I express if my baby still isn't nursing by Day Three?
output: If your baby isn’t nursing by Day Three, you can continue to hand-express or use a rental-grade pump. It’s helpful to express about fifteen minutes on each side every two to three hours, using breast massage to encourage more milk.

instruction: Is colostrum easier to hand-express or pump in the first few days?
output: In the first few days colostrum is thick and is usually easier to remove by hand expression than by pumping. Massage and breast compressions also help, and many mothers can express more colostrum in the first hours after birth than they do later.

instruction: My baby seems to act frustrated and pulls away from the breast — could that explain the poor latch?
output: A baby who won’t breastfeed at the moment can show frustration by pulling away. This may be the baby's way of expressing strong feelings. The key is patience and skilled help; your baby can learn to nurse but it may take time and gentle, consistent support.

instruction: Are rental-quality breast pumps available in hospitals if I need one by Day Three?
output: Many hospitals can provide access to rental-grade pumps or point you to local sources. You can continue to hand-express or use a rental pump if your baby isn’t nursing by Day Three.

instruction: When can I take a break from expressing if my baby isn’t nursing yet?
output: After an initial expressing session you can take about a six-hour break, then begin expressing every two to three hours around the clock (about eight to ten times in 24 hours).

instruction: Why is a flat pillow sometimes recommended over a rounded one for positioning small babies?
output: A rounded pillow can create a crease where a small baby tends to roll into, so a flat-surfaced pillow may work better and be less likely to cause positioning problems.

instruction: What is the first of the 'Three Keeps' when breastfeeding isn't working?
output: The first 'Keep' is to keep your milk flowing. Early on your breasts are planning for future milk production and the amount of milk you remove tells them how much you’ll need. If the baby isn’t nursing, start hand-expressing or pumping, use massage and breast compressions, and express frequently so your breasts are ready when the baby can nurse.

instruction: Can the effects of labor medications on a baby's ability to nurse last more than a day?
output: Yes. For some babies the effects of labor medications or interventions can last for days or weeks; other babies may have physical or neurological difficulties that affect nursing.

instruction: My baby isn't nursing yet. How can I keep my milk supply ready and growing so I'll be able to breastfeed when they are?
output: Your milk supply will begin to increase after the first couple of days, but the early days are when your breasts plan for future production and the amount you remove now helps determine future supply. If your baby isn't nursing, start hand-expressing or pumping now. Colostrum is thick and usually easier to remove by hand expression than by pumping. Use massage and breast compressions and ask for help expressing. After an initial break of about six hours, begin expressing every two to three hours around the clock (about eight to ten times in 24 hours). If your baby still isn't nursing by Day Three, continue hand-expressing or get a rental-grade pump; express about 15 minutes on each side every two to three hours and use massage to encourage more milk.

instruction: My baby only gets tiny drops of colostrum. How can I feed those drops to my baby?
output: A little colostrum goes a long way. Hand-express into a plastic spoon and tip the drops into your baby's mouth. Have an assembly line so one spoon is being filled while another is being used. If you have more than drops, collect from the spoon with an eyedropper or medicine dropper. If expressing into a pump funnel, you can wipe drops off the funnel with your finger and let the baby suck them off, or collect from the pump's bottle with an eyedropper.

instruction: How often and how long should I express milk in the first days if my baby can't nurse yet?
output: After about a six-hour break, begin expressing every two to three hours around the clock (about eight to ten times in 24 hours). It is helpful to express for about 15 minutes on each side and use breast massage to encourage more milk.

instruction: Does skin-to-skin contact help a baby who isn't yet organized enough to breastfeed?
output: Bare skin against your chest helps encourage breastfeeding in a baby who is still getting organized. Keep a covering over both of you so you stay warm; your heart and breathing will help pace and steady the baby, and having your breast nearby gives the baby access to feeding when ready. Skin-to-skin also stabilizes and comforts the baby.

instruction: My baby mouths my nipple but won't suckle. What can I try to get him to start sucking?
output: Compress your breast between your thumb and fingers so that a few drops gather on your nipple; those drops may encourage him to start sucking.

instruction: Can I let my baby doze on my chest or at my breast, or should I avoid that until he can really nurse?
output: You can let him doze on your chest or at your breast as much as you like. Having relaxed, low-pressure feeding times and keeping your breast nearby helps remind him that your chest is a satisfying place and encourages breastfeeding while comforting and stabilizing the baby.

instruction: I've heard about mothers licking their babies. Why do mammals lick newborns, and does it ever help in humans?
output: All mammals lick their babies after birth to clean them and to stimulate breathing, digestion, and neural responses; licking also shows affection and enhances the sense of smell. Human mothers don't typically lick their babies, but some who have done so have found that their babies responded well.

instruction: If my baby hasn't nursed by several days of age even with skilled help, what else could I try to help breastfeeding start?
output: If your baby hasn't nursed by several days even with skilled help, you could try a nipple shield. Keep feeding times relaxed, happy, and low-pressure; play at breastfeeding when you both feel like it. Time, trust in the process, and a skilled helper are your best allies; many babies begin nursing, though it may take several weeks or more.

instruction: What should I do with the first large amount of colostrum some mothers can express in the hours after birth?
output: Many mothers can express more colostrum in the first hours after birth than later; this can be fed by spoon, eyedropper, or other supportive feeding methods if the baby isn't nursing yet. Ask for help expressing if needed.

instruction: When should I consider renting a pump if my baby still isn't nursing?
output: If your baby isn't nursing by Day Three, when your milk volume often starts to increase, you can continue hand-expressing or get a rental-grade pump. Many hospitals have rental-grade pumps or can point you to local sources.

instruction: Could giving lots of food at the very beginning affect my baby's future feeding needs?
output: There is some evidence that giving large amounts of formula or other feedings early on may increase a baby's later demand. A little colostrum goes a long way.

instruction: How can I use a pump funnel and bottle to feed my baby small amounts of expressed milk?
output: If you're expressing into a pump funnel or pumping, you can wipe drops off the funnel with your finger and let your baby suck them off. Collect colostrum from the pump's bottle with an eyedropper or medicine dropper to feed small amounts.

instruction: What support should I keep in place while I'm working on breastfeeding?
output: Keep in touch with someone who understands breastfeeding and whose approach you respect. A book can give general information, but for real help you need someone who understands breastfeeding personally and can provide skilled help and encouragement.

instruction: My newborn just mouths my nipple but won't suckle. What can I try to encourage him to start sucking?
output: Compress your breast between your thumb and fingers so a few drops gather on the nipple to encourage sucking. Also keep your baby close: let him doze on your chest or at your breast and offer relaxed, low-pressure feeding times to encourage breastfeeding.

instruction: Is it okay to let my baby doze on my chest or at my breast?
output: Yes. Letting your baby doze on your chest or at your breast can encourage breastfeeding and helps stabilize and comfort your baby.

instruction: I’ve heard some mothers lick their babies after birth. What does that do and do babies respond?
output: Mammals lick newborns to clean and stimulate them and to enhance smell and bonding. Some human mothers who have licked their babies have reported positive responses, though it is not common practice.

instruction: My baby still hasn't nursed after several days even with help. What options are suggested to try?
output: You could try a nipple shield and keep the Three Keeps in mind: keep your milk flowing, keep your baby fed, and keep your baby close. Stay in touch with a knowledgeable breastfeeding helper such as a La Leche League Leader or an IBCLC.

instruction: How should I approach feeding times if breastfeeding is not going smoothly?
output: Make feeding times relaxed, happy, and low-pressure. Play at breastfeeding when you both feel like it rather than making it a stress-filled task. Time, trust in the process, and a skilled helper are your best allies.

instruction: What qualities should I look for in a breastfeeding helper?
output: A good helper should ask questions before giving ideas; be kind and respectful to you and your baby; be endlessly patient; support your choices and goals; know other people and places to turn to; have up-to-date knowledge; adjust ideas to your situation; be ready to change suggestions that aren’t working; leave you feeling stronger; and stick with you as long as you want.

instruction: Where can I find a local breastfeeding helper or consultant?
output: A good place to start is La Leche League if there is a Group or Leader in your community. You can also check ilca.org for International Board Certified Lactation Consultants (IBCLCs) near you. Larger cities often have more resources.

instruction: How long might it take for my baby to start nursing?
output: Many babies begin nursing within days or weeks, but for some it can take several weeks or more. Babies have started at a range of ages; patience and support help.

instruction: I'm really worried breastfeeding will never happen for us. Should I give up?
output: It’s understandable to worry. Many families succeed with time and support. In the meantime there are ways to keep your milk supply strong and supports to seek out. Keep working with a skilled helper and consider supplemental feeding methods while you continue.

instruction: Can premature or small or sick babies breastfeed?
output: Yes. Early babies, small babies, and sick babies are all capable of feeding at the breast, though they may need extra time and skilled support.

instruction: How can I get better at breastfeeding — are there tips for practice?
output: Practice makes progress. You and your baby will have many opportunities to practice through frequent breastfeeding. Over time you'll find positions and styles that fit you both.

instruction: If a suggestion from a breastfeeding helper isn't working, what should they do?
output: A good helper should be ready to change any suggestion that isn’t working and adjust information and ideas to your situation.

instruction: Are there recommended books for latching approaches I can read?
output: Recommended books include Breastfeeding Made Simple by Nancy Mohrbacher and Kathleen Kendall-Tackett; The Latch by Jack Newman and Teresa Pitman; Mother-Baby Experiences of Nurturing by Suzanne Colson; and Supporting Sucking Skills in Breastfeeding Infants edited by Catherine Watson Genna.

instruction: What are the "Three Keeps" I should remember while breastfeeding?
output: The Three Keeps are: Keep your milk flowing; Keep your baby fed; and Keep your baby close. Also stay in touch with someone who really understands breastfeeding for ongoing support.

instruction: My baby isn't latching reliably yet. Will they ever learn to latch, or should I be worried?
output: Many babies learn to latch with time and support. Frequent breastfeeding gives lots of practice; staying patient and working with a skilled helper increases the chances of success.

instruction: How long does it usually take to get the hang of breastfeeding?
output: It takes time for you and your baby to get the hang of breastfeeding. Because you’ll nurse frequently, you’ll have many opportunities to practice and improve.

instruction: My newborn was born early — can an early baby breastfeed? I'm scared they won't be able to.
output: Babies are built to breastfeed. Early, small, or sick babies may need extra help and time, but many can and do learn to feed at the breast with appropriate support.

instruction: Where will I be able to nurse once we figure it out? I worry about being stuck sitting in one awkward chair.
output: You can nurse in many places: chairs, sofas, parks, a parked car, lying on your side in bed, or sitting on the floor. Over time you'll develop positions that suit your body and your baby’s preferences.

instruction: While we're working on the latch, what can I do about my milk supply?
output: There are ways to keep your milk supply strong while working on latch, including frequent hand expression or pumping, massage, and breast compressions. Seek help from a skilled breastfeeding helper for personalized guidance.

instruction: I haven't found a comfortable nursing position yet. Will I ever find one that fits me?
output: You may find a style tailored to your breast shape, body, and your baby’s preferences. With time you and your baby will develop positions that feel comfortable.

instruction: Are there recommended resources for different latching approaches I can read or watch?
output: Yes. Recommended resources include: Breastfeeding Made Simple (book); The Latch (book); Supporting Sucking Skills in Breastfeeding Infants (technical book); Baby-Led Breastfeeding (DVD); Biological Nurturing: Laid-back Breastfeeding (DVD); and Follow Me Mum (DVD).

instruction: Do La Leche League Leaders ever think a mother should stop trying to get a latch?
output: Leaders who support breastfeeding generally focus on keeping a mother’s faith and offering ongoing support until the baby starts nursing, rather than advising a mother to stop trying prematurely.

instruction: Will I eventually be able to breastfeed without needing books or constant help?
output: Yes. Over time you and your baby will gain skill and confidence and you will likely need less help and be able to put the books away.

instruction: Can I breastfeed lying on my side in bed, or do I have to sit up?
output: Yes, you can breastfeed lying on your side in bed. Many other positions (sitting cross-legged, reclining, etc.) also work—choose what is comfortable and safe for you and your baby.

instruction: Are there technical books for helpers on supporting sucking skills?
output: Yes. Supporting Sucking Skills in Breastfeeding Infants, edited by Catherine Watson Genna, is an example of a more technical book for breastfeeding helpers.

instruction: Do any of the recommended resources come as DVDs if I prefer watching to reading?
output: Yes. Some recommended DVDs include Baby-Led Breastfeeding, Biological Nurturing: Laid-back Breastfeeding, and Follow Me Mum.

instruction: How long should I do skin-to-skin with my newborn right after birth?
output: For at least the first two hours after birth, keep your baby bare or diaper-only on your bare chest for uninterrupted skin-to-skin contact when possible.

instruction: Do I need to have my baby weighed and get ointments right away, or can we wait?
output: Routine measurements, ointments, and similar procedures can usually wait; the first couple of hours are prime time for skin-to-skin contact and getting breastfeeding started.

instruction: What's the right way to position my baby for skin-to-skin?
output: Place the baby chest-to-chest on your bare chest. Open your shirt or gown, lean back comfortably so gravity holds the baby against you, and cover both of you with a regular blanket to keep warm.

instruction: What are the benefits of doing skin-to-skin with my baby right after birth?
output: Skin-to-skin helps stabilize the baby’s heart rate, breathing, and temperature; supports maternal temperature regulation; can reduce maternal postpartum blues; reduces the baby’s stress and pain from procedures; reduces parental stress; increases interaction; and improves the likelihood and duration of breastfeeding.

instruction: How much will my baby eat in the first days? It seems very little — is that normal?
output: Yes. The colostrum your baby gets at first is small in quantity, which matches the baby’s small stomach size (roughly the size of a large marble at birth).

instruction: My baby had sticky black poop — is that normal and will it stain cloth diapers?
output: The baby's first stool, meconium, is sticky and black and is normal. It can stain cloth diapers, so some parents prefer disposables until the meconium has passed.

instruction: How long will my postpartum bleeding last and what should I plan for?
output: Postpartum bleeding (lochia) is often like a heavy menstrual flow and can last a couple of weeks. At times the flow can be heavy, so you may want overnight pads and to keep a pad or dark-colored towel on the bed initially.

instruction: I stood up and had a big gush of blood — is that expected?
output: A noticeable gush when you first stand up can happen in the early postpartum days as blood and fluid move; this can be a normal occurrence. If bleeding becomes excessively heavy or you have other concerning symptoms, contact your provider.

instruction: When I breastfeed, I sometimes feel strong cramps and a gush of blood — is that normal?
output: Yes. Breastfeeding releases oxytocin which causes uterine contractions; you may notice cramps and occasional gushes of blood during feeds. The cramps usually fade over time, though they can be strong at first.

instruction: What can I try when my newborn fusses and breastfeeding doesn't calm him?
output: Try breastfeeding first. If that doesn't work, try walking with him, talking or singing, a gentle bounce, or taking him outside. Different babies respond to different soothing techniques.

instruction: How will I learn what my baby needs — are there signs I can look for?
output: Babies give cues: they may hold still before peeing, squirm and rub fists against their face when tired, or bob their head against your chest when hungry. Over time you'll learn to read these signals.

instruction: Is it okay to put my baby in a sling and go for a short walk soon after birth?
output: Yes. When the weather is suitable, wearing your baby in a sling for short walks can feel good; fresh air and gentle movement often help both parent and baby.

instruction: I'm worried because my recovery was harder — I had surgery and breastfeeding isn't easy. Will things get better?
output: It's understandable to feel concerned. Recovery from surgery can make the early days harder, and you may experience effects of medications or delayed milk. With time, support, and skilled help, many families work through these early challenges.

instruction: I want my baby to stay close — is there any reason not to keep him in my sight a lot?
output: There's no general reason to keep your baby out of sight in the early days. Keeping your baby close helps you notice cues, interact more, and support feeding and bonding.

instruction: Why are there so few wet diapers at first after birth?
output: Newborns receive small amounts of colostrum and have very small stomachs, so there are usually few wet diapers in the first day or two.

instruction: My baby’s first poop is black and sticky — is that normal and will it stain cloth diapers?
output: Yes. The first stool (meconium) is sticky and black and consists of in-utero intestinal contents. It can stain cloth, so many people use disposables until the meconium is gone.

instruction: How long does postpartum bleeding (lochia) last and what should I have on hand at home?
output: Lochia can last for a couple of weeks and may be heavy at times. Keep overnight pads and a dark towel or pad for the bed handy during the early days.

instruction: When I breastfeed, I get cramping and sometimes a gush of blood — is that expected?
output: Yes. Oxytocin released during breastfeeding causes uterine contractions that can produce cramps and occasional gushes of blood; these cramps usually fade with time.

instruction: I feel starving after giving birth — is that normal?
output: Yes. It's common to be very hungry after delivery because childbirth is physically demanding. Unless you are restricted for medical reasons, plan to eat satisfying meals after birth.

instruction: I can’t sleep well after the baby is born — what causes that and will keeping the baby with me help?
output: You may sleep lightly because pregnancy hormones drop quickly after birth and new-parent alertness is high. Keeping your baby with you (rooming-in) often helps you get more restorative sleep overall.

instruction: I’m overwhelmed by visitors in the hospital — what can I do to limit visits?
output: Ask hospital staff to stop visitors at reception or to suggest visiting when you get home. At home, set short visiting hours, post a note on the door, or use a plan that directs visitors' energy (for example, a list on the refrigerator).

instruction: I’m leaking urine when I cough and my shirts get soaked with milk — is this normal and how long will it last?
output: Leaking is common after birth. Some women have temporary urinary leakage with coughing or sneezing, and early milk production can make shirts wet. These issues are usually temporary.

instruction: I’ve been crying a lot and feel emotional for days — should I be worried?
output: Feeling tearful and emotional in the early postpartum days is common. If you find you are feeling anxious or depressed for several days at a time, talk to your doctor or midwife as soon as possible because it could be postpartum depression.

instruction: I had a lot of IV fluids during labor and still look puffy — is that expected and will it affect my milk?
output: Yes. Large IV fluid volumes can cause temporary puffiness of the face, hands, and breasts. It can take several weeks to return to normal and heavy IV fluids may be associated with a delayed increase in milk volume. Eating protein or hydrating with certain foods like watermelon may help reduce swelling.

instruction: Any tips for getting through the first few nights at home with a newborn?
output: Take plenty of naps, including an evening nap, and prepare a bedside 'nest.' Keep easy snacks and water beside the bed so you don't have to manage packaging at 3 a.m., and keep essentials within reach.

instruction: Will my baby suddenly start needing lots of diaper changes once milk comes in?
output: Yes. Once your milk comes in, your baby will usually have more wet and dirty diapers, so expect to change more diapers than in the very first days.

instruction: Does increasing interaction with my baby help breastfeeding last longer?
output: Yes. Increasing interactions between you and your baby is associated with greater likelihood and longer duration of breastfeeding.

instruction: I don’t feel immediate love for my baby and I feel guilty — could that change?
output: It's common not to feel immediate intense love. Some people feel love immediately; for others it grows over weeks to months. Either pattern can be normal and many people find their feelings deepen with time.

instruction: What is meconium and why is the first stool different?
output: Meconium is the sticky black first stool made up of materials that filled the baby's intestines before birth, which is why the first stools look different from later ones.

instruction: I'm still very swollen after delivery. Could that be from IV fluids, and what might help reduce the swelling?
output: Yes. Large IV fluid volumes can cause post-birth swelling of the eyes, fingers, and breasts. Eating protein or hydrating with foods like watermelon may help reduce excess fluid. If you were on an IV for several days, give yourself weeks to recover and note there may be a delay in milk volume increase.

instruction: I've been feeling anxious and down for several days. What should I do?
output: If you are feeling anxious or depressed for days at a time, contact your doctor or midwife as soon as possible. This could be postpartum depression and early support can help you recover and enjoy your baby.

instruction: How long should I let my newborn nurse in the first few days?
output: Let your baby nurse as long as he wants. An unmedicated baby may nurse for an hour or more at a time in the first few days; a medicated or sleepy baby may need encouragement to stay awake and nurse longer.

instruction: My baby is very sleepy from labor medications. How often should I wake him to feed?
output: If your baby is too sedated from birth medications or jaundiced to wake on his own, wake him every three hours or so, starting about six hours after birth. Aim for at least eight feedings in 24 hours, counting from the beginning of one feeding to the beginning of the next.

instruction: Can I switch breasts whenever I get uncomfortable while nursing?
output: Yes. In the first few days, if your nipple, arm, or nose becomes uncomfortable and you want to switch before the baby finishes, that's fine. Use both breasts during the day but don't worry about exact equality of time.

instruction: My nipples hurt and I'm not sure the baby should nurse so long. What can I do?
output: Nipples are generally resilient, but if you need relief, seek help from a La Leche League leader or another experienced breastfeeding helper to problem-solve and find more comfortable positions or techniques.

instruction: How do I break my baby's suction safely if I need to get up during a feed?
output: Slip a finger into the corner of the baby’s mouth to break the suction. Use a finger about the size of your nipple, slide it into the corner between the gums, and twist gently as you would open a paint can lid; then slide your nipple out safely.

instruction: I had an IV for several days — could that delay my milk coming in?
output: Yes. Being on IV fluids for several days can be associated with taking longer to return to your baseline and a possible delay in the volume increase of milk. Give your body time and continue frequent expression and feeding attempts.

instruction: I don't feel an immediate rush of love for my baby. Is that normal?
output: Yes. Some people feel love immediately while for others it grows over weeks to months. Both experiences are common and many people find their feelings deepen with time and bonding.

instruction: Any practical tips for handling the first few nights at home with a newborn?
output: Take naps when you can, prepare a comfortable bedside area, keep easy snacks and water within reach, and plan for overnight care so you can rest between feedings.

instruction: Can keeping my baby in the bed with me help if he has jaundice?
output: Keeping the baby close and feeding frequently (rooming-in) can increase feeding frequency and help some babies with jaundice by improving intake. Discuss specifics with your care team to ensure safe monitoring and follow-up.

instruction: What should I do if my baby falls asleep after only a few minutes of nursing?
output: If he falls asleep or stops nursing actively within a few minutes, you can try to rouse and encourage him to continue. If he's too drowsy to manage sustained feeding, hand expression to provide milk or express to supplement can help in the first few days.

instruction: My baby got pain meds during delivery and won't wake to nurse. When should I wake him to feed?
output: If your baby is too sedated from birth medications or jaundiced to wake on his own, wake him every three hours or so, starting about six hours after birth, aiming for at least eight feedings in 24 hours.

instruction: How can I safely break my baby's suction if I need to get up for a moment?
output: Slide a finger into the corner of your baby’s mouth to break the suction. Use a finger about the size of your nipple, slide it into the corner between the gums, and twist gently before removing the nipple.

instruction: Can I keep nursing on the first breast until either of us wants to stop?
output: Yes. If your baby is latched and comfortable, nurse from the first breast until one of you wants to stop. Sometimes the baby will take the other breast afterward, and sometimes he won't; both are fine.

instruction: My newborn falls asleep after a few minutes of nursing. Should I try to get him to keep going?
output: If he falls asleep or stops nursing actively within a few minutes, you can encourage him to continue. If he remains too drowsy to feed well, hand expression can help provide milk in the first few days.

instruction: What can I do if my baby is too fuzzy to nurse well in the first days?
output: Hand expression can help provide milk and support feeding when a baby is too sleepy or 'fuzzy' to nurse effectively in the first days.

instruction: Is it harmful if the hospital keeps my baby in a central nursery away from me at night?
output: Having your baby away from you can reduce opportunities for feeding cues, may slow milk coming in, and can make it harder for you to control supplementation and monitor the baby. Many families find rooming-in supports feeding and bonding.

instruction: What does rooming-in mean in the hospital?
output: Rooming-in means the baby is kept in the same room as the mother, in a bassinet or Isolette, allowing the baby to be held, fed, and comforted by the mother more easily during the day.

instruction: What is a sidecar bed in the hospital?
output: A sidecar bed is a baby bed attached to the mother’s hospital bed so the baby is placed next to the mother instead of in a separate bassinet.

instruction: What does bedding-in mean and what does it do?
output: Bedding-in means mother and baby share the same bed day and night, nursing as they choose. It can help synchronize sleep patterns and may support a faster, more abundant milk supply for some families. Safe sleep practices must be followed.

instruction: I'm taking painkillers after delivery. Is it safe to sleep with my baby in bed with me?
output: If you are using painkillers, sleeping pills, or other medications that make you drowsy or alter your awareness, your baby will be safer in someone else’s arms or in a bedside bassinet when you plan to sleep.

instruction: How can a double bed make sharing sleep safer in the hospital?
output: Some hospitals offer double beds so a mother, partner, and baby can sleep together with the baby placed between the two adults at breast height, reducing the need for pillows near the baby and improving safety when adults are awake and attentive.

instruction: Can I ask nurses for different sleeping or feeding arrangements in the hospital?
output: Yes. Discuss your preferences with a nurse and, if you feel mismatched with one nurse, you have the right to request a different nurse or to speak with another staff member about arrangements.

instruction: I heard about hospitals getting rid of bassinets because of jaundice problems. What happened?
output: Some hospitals have found that placing babies in mothers' beds or rooming-in, with frequent feeding and positioning support, improved feeding and reduced jaundice and related problems. Staff then adjusted care practices to support mothers and babies.

instruction: How many times should a newborn feed in 24 hours and when should I start timing feedings?
output: Start timing from the beginning of one feeding to the beginning of the next about six hours after birth, and aim for at least eight feedings in twenty-four hours.

instruction: If I need to step away briefly while my baby is latched, will he want to nurse again when I come back?
output: You can slip a finger into the corner of his mouth to break the suction and do what you need to do; your baby may want to nurse again once you come back, and you can offer the breast if he seems interested.

instruction: Can I keep my newborn skin-to-skin in the hospital during the day?
output: Yes. Rooming-in is common: you can have your baby on your chest, in your arms, or at your side as much as you like during the day. Many hospitals place the baby in a bassinet or Isolette in the same room, and some offer sidecar beds as alternatives.

instruction: What are the benefits of 'bedding-in' (sharing the same bed with my baby)?
output: Bedding-in means mother and baby share the same bed day and night. It lets you nurse whenever you choose, helps your sleep patterns begin to synchronize with the baby's, encourages bonding, and is associated with a faster, more abundant milk supply.

instruction: Can my baby sleep in my hospital bed at night?
output: Many hospitals discourage a mother having her baby in her bed at night. Some hospitals use a sidecar bed attached to the mother's bed instead, and rooming-in usually places the baby in a bassinet or Isolette in the same room.

instruction: I'm taking painkillers after delivery. Is it safe to share sleep with my baby in the hospital?
output: If you are using painkillers, sleeping pills, or other consciousness-altering medications, your baby will be safer in someone else's arms or in a bedside bassinet when you feel drowsy or plan to sleep.

instruction: Do any hospitals offer beds that make co-sleeping with a partner easier and safer?
output: Yes. Some hospitals now have double beds that let mother, partner, and baby sleep together more comfortably by placing the baby between the two adults at breast height so pillows aren't an issue.

instruction: If I don't like my nurse's approach to rooming-in, can I ask for someone else?
output: Yes. Discuss arrangements with staff and, if the match isn't working, you can request a different nurse or ask for changes in your care plan.

instruction: How worried should I be about giving my newborn a little formula in the first days?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Early formula can affect the newborn intestinal environment that is being established by your milk; giving formula in the first days is not a trivial choice because it can influence the baby's gut bacteria and initial protection.

instruction: If my baby gets a little formula now, how long can it affect their intestinal bacteria?
output: A little formula after the early days can alter your baby's protective intestinal bacteria for a full month. Also, formula in the first few months before the intestines are mature may increase allergy and diabetes risk in susceptible children; the risk may be highest in the first few days and in babies born even a little early.

instruction: What can hospitals offer if my milk isn't available right after birth?
output: If your milk isn't available, banked human colostrum or mature donor milk is the next best choice. More hospitals keep banked milk on hand; ask your birthplace in advance what they offer and whether donor milk is available.

instruction: What should I expect for my full-term baby's pees and poos in the first week?
output: In the first 24 hours, a slightly wet diaper and a black, tarry stool (meconium) are normal. By the second day the baby will usually pass most meconium and make a bit more urine. By the third day you should see real changes in stool color, and by the end of the week you will likely see yellow stools and noticeably heavier diapers.

instruction: I see orange-red specks in my newborn's diaper on day three — is that bleeding?
output: Those orange-red deposits are commonly called brick dust or urate crystals. They are highly concentrated urine from limited fluid intake and are very common in breastfed babies on the third day before the milk fully comes in; they will usually disappear as milk volume increases. They are often mistaken for bleeding after a baby boy's circumcision or for a baby girl's normal vaginal bleeding from pregnancy hormones.

instruction: When should I contact someone about orange-red deposits in the diaper?
output: If the orange-red deposits continue after the fifth day, especially if your baby is having fewer than three poops a day, contact your baby's doctor and your breastfeeding helper.

instruction: Does being born by Cesarean change my baby's need for my milk or presence?
output: Babies born by Cesarean may not acquire the same bacteria they would during vaginal birth, can be exposed to more hospital bacteria, and may particularly benefit from extra parental presence and breast milk in the early days.

instruction: Do I need to record every feeding and diaper change in the hospital?
output: You may be tempted to track many details, but in the very early days focus on patterns like diaper output and feeding effectiveness. Once your baby has a solid growth pattern, continuous detailed logging is usually unnecessary.

instruction: How should my full-term baby's diapers look in the first 24 hours?
output: A full-term baby having a slightly wet diaper and a black, tarry stool in the first 24 hours is normal and doing fine.

instruction: What changes in poop and urine should I expect by day 2 and day 3?
output: By the second 24 hours your baby will usually get enough colostrum to pass most of the meconium and make a bit more urine. By the third day you should see real changes in stool color as transitional stools replace meconium.

instruction: When should I expect yellow poop and heavier diapers?
output: By the end of the first week you will probably see yellow stools and noticeably heavier diapers as your milk supply increases.

instruction: Why doesn't the newborn chart record pees?
output: Some charts don't record pees because individual wet diapers in the first few days are less informative; clinicians usually look for overall patterns of feeding, stooling, and weight rather than counting every pee early on.

instruction: How much weight loss after birth is considered acceptable?
output: Conventional guidance is that babies generally shouldn't lose more than about 7% of birth weight before regaining, should not exceed a 10% loss, and are usually back to birth weight by two weeks. Be aware that maternal IV fluids during labor can affect apparent birth weight.

instruction: My baby lost a good amount of weight but is eager to feed, swallowing well, and producing more diapers—should I worry?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure. If your baby is active, organized at feeds, swallowing well, and starting to produce more diapers, he is probably doing fine; continue to watch that good progress continues.

instruction: My baby hasn't lost much weight but is sleepy, not swallowing, and not pooping — what should I do?
output: If your baby is sleepy, not swallowing, and not pooping, he probably needs help. You may need to feed him more often or express extra milk to increase intake and to stimulate your breasts. Check with a breastfeeding helper (e.g., an LLL Leader) or your doctor if you don't see more pooping within a day or two.

instruction: Are issues like hypothermia, hypoglycemia, jaundice, engorgement, and excessive weight loss linked to anything specific in the early days?
output: Those problems are commonly related to early sleepiness and separation from the mother, which can reduce feeding and temperature regulation.

instruction: How can I best avoid early sleepiness and separation for my newborn?
output: Keeping your baby skin-to-skin or on/against your body most of the time in the early days and supporting frequent feeding helps avoid early sleepiness and separation; unmedicated birth may also reduce the baby's initial sleepiness.

instruction: I'm nervous about going home alone with a newborn — will the baby break if I hold him wrong or skip baths for a while?
output: Your baby won't break if you hold him imperfectly, and it's fine to delay bathing; babies are resilient and parental instincts and common sense generally guide safe care. It's normal to feel unsure.

instruction: Can maternal IV fluids during labor affect my baby's apparent weight loss after birth?
output: Yes. A mother who had a lot of IV fluids may deliver a baby who appears to weigh more at birth and thus seems to lose more weight afterward; this can make weight-loss measurements misleading.

instruction: My newborn sleeps for 4–5 hours at a stretch. Should I wake him to feed?
output: If you had no birth medications, your baby stays in physical contact with you, he eats well when he eats, and he is gaining well, then sleeping longer than three hours is often just his pattern. Keep an eye on his weight and feeding effectiveness.

instruction: Every time I put my baby down after nursing he screams and looks for the breast. Why is he doing this?
output: Your baby has discovered that the breast is the most comforting place — close to the womb experience. He may protest being taken off and root for the breast as a way of settling and comforting, even when your milk is available.

instruction: When my baby dozes at the breast, should I move him or try to burp him?
output: When he drifts off at the breast, don’t move him except to pillow his head more comfortably. Don’t try to burp him; snuggle until he reaches a deeper sleep with even breathing, or keep holding him.

instruction: Is it okay to keep my newborn against my body most of the time in the early days?
output: Yes. Keeping your baby on or against your body most of the time in the early days is a helpful way to avoid sleepiness and separation and to encourage frequent feeding.

instruction: Should I take my newborn's mittens off or leave them on so he won't scratch himself?
output: Take the mittens off. Babies may scratch themselves, but their skin heals quickly and leaving mittens on can interfere with feeding cues and hand use.

instruction: My baby makes weird throat noises and coughs sometimes. Is that normal?
output: Yes. Newborns commonly make strange sounds because their airway and throat reflexes are immature; occasional noises and coughs are usually normal in the early days.

instruction: Can I skip bathing my newborn for a while if I'm tired or worried about water temperature?
output: Yes. You can safely delay bathing for days or weeks if needed; a slightly cool bath is also unlikely to harm a healthy newborn. Prioritize rest and feeding.

instruction: What causes the 'second night' wakefulness and lots of crying?
output: On the second night a baby may suddenly react to being outside the womb — new noises, lights, smells, and handling can be scary. Babies may discover their voice, root, and cry for comfort and the breast as they adjust.

instruction: Does my baby touching my breast while nursing do anything for my milk?
output: Yes. A baby’s touch on your breast increases maternal oxytocin and can help with milk release and the breastfeeding process, as well as providing comfort for the baby.

instruction: Are questions about newborn worries silly to ask leaders or other parents?
output: No question is silly. Many mothers contact breastfeeding leaders or experienced parents about common issues in the first few days; getting answers helps you become confident and fluent in caring for your baby.

instruction: If my baby sleeps more than three hours and we were separated after birth, should I be concerned?
output: A baby who was separated from the mother may shut down to conserve energy and sleep longer than usual; in those cases sleeping more than three hours can indicate the need for closer monitoring and possible intervention. Keep an eye on weight and feeding.

instruction: Which early problems are linked to sleepiness and separation?
output: Hypothermia, hypoglycemia, jaundice, engorgement, and excessive infant weight loss are all problems that can be related to early sleepiness and separation from the mother.

instruction: If I had an epidural, could that affect how easily my baby wakes to feed?
output: Yes. A baby whose mother had birth medications such as an epidural may be harder to rouse and may sleep longer than usual for a while, which can affect feeding frequency.

instruction: What should I do if my baby roots and seems to want the breast after I've put him down?
output: That’s fine — it’s his way of settling and comforting. If he starts rooting and seems to want to go back to the breast, let him; extra snuggling at the breast often helps.

instruction: Is it normal for a newborn to startle, cough, or sneeze a lot in the first days?
output: Yes. Startling, coughing, and sneezing are common newborn reflexes and are usually normal in the first days.

instruction: I’m worried my baby sleeps too much and isn’t getting enough milk. What signs in the first days should I watch?
output: Watch that he eats effectively when he feeds and that his weight is tracking appropriately. If you had birth medications or the baby was separated, be especially vigilant; contact a breastfeeding helper or your pediatrician if weight or output is not improving.

instruction: My newborn sleeps for 4 hours at a stretch — should I wake him to feed?
output: If you had no birth medications, your baby is in contact with you, eats well when he does feed, and is gaining appropriately, long stretches can be his pattern; keep an eye on weight. If you had an epidural or other birth medications and your baby isn’t waking at least every two to three hours, assume medications may be prolonging sleep and offer feeds or expressed milk.

instruction: I had an epidural — how often should my newborn be roused to feed?
output: If you had an epidural or other birth medications and your baby isn’t waking at least every two or three hours, it’s safer to rouse him and get food into him, since medications can keep him sleeping more than usual.

instruction: What signs show my baby is in a wake-able time?
output: Wake-able times include when you feel a little tension in an arm as you lift it, when you see eye movement under the lids, when the mouth makes little sucking movements, or when the baby is making other small movements; these are easier times to wake and feed.

instruction: How can I gently wake my baby for a feeding?
output: Try dimming bright light, undressing or putting the baby skin-to-skin on your chest, stroking and talking softly, rubbing feet, or wiping the face with a damp cloth. If those don’t work, try laying the baby on the bed on the back and gently rolling side to side, or hold along your forearms and lift and lower while talking gently.

instruction: Is it okay to put a little colostrum or milk in my baby's mouth to wake her?
output: As a last resort you can put a little colostrum or milk in her mouth, waiting for her to swallow before adding more; some babies will sleep through a meal or two and may need this to stimulate feeding.

instruction: My baby latches and seems to nurse while still asleep — is that okay?
output: It’s fine if your baby latches and nurses in her sleep, especially in a laid-back breastfeeding position. The practical goal is to help her take in milk; some babies feed effectively while drowsy.

instruction: The hospital brought my baby back swaddled — what should I do when I get her?
output: If your baby comes back swaddled, unwrap him and snuggle his bare or lightly clothed skin against yours with your blankets over both of you; he will quickly regain heat and you can keep him with you.

instruction: Is swaddling safe for a newborn?
output: Swaddling can calm some infants, but for ordinary newborns it has risks. Research suggests swaddled newborns may have poorer circulation, lower temperature, and slower initial weight gain, possibly because they signal hunger less effectively. Swaddling also limits movement, and sleeping while swaddled can increase risk. It is generally not recommended to swaddle routinely in the first hours after birth.

instruction: Should I swaddle my baby in the first two hours after birth?
output: No. It’s best not to swaddle an ordinary baby in the first two hours after birth because early skin contact and ease of feeding are important for bonding and initiating breastfeeding.

instruction: Is it safe for a swaddled baby to sleep with me in bed?
output: While many mothers sleep safely with their babies when appropriate precautions are taken, sleeping with a swaddled baby is riskier because the swaddled infant cannot move to protect their airway. Avoid bed-sharing with a swaddled baby.

instruction: Why might swaddling cause slower weight gain in newborns?
output: Swaddled babies may gain weight more slowly because they are less able to signal hunger and may be less aware of hunger cues, which can reduce feeding frequency.

instruction: My baby slept through a whole feeding — should I be worried?
output: Occasionally a baby will sleep through a meal or two; some babies will also latch and feed while drowsy. The primary goal is to ensure they take in enough milk overall, so monitor feeding effectiveness and diaper output.

instruction: Are nipples supposed to hurt when breastfeeding?
output: Breastfeeding is not supposed to hurt. Nipples should not crack, bleed, or come out of the baby's mouth damaged. Mild sensitivity or stinging for a day or two can occur as things settle, but persistent pain indicates a problem that should be addressed.

instruction: If my baby was separated from me after birth, why might he sleep more than usual?
output: Separation from the mother can lead a baby to conserve energy and sleep more than usual. Birth medications in the mother can also make a baby harder to rouse. Both situations warrant closer monitoring of feeding and output.

instruction: Will undressing my baby help wake him for a feeding?
output: Undressing may help wake him; if not, skin-to-skin contact on your chest or other gentle stimulation can help rouse him for feeding.

instruction: Is it okay to keep my newborn against my body most of the time in the early days?
output: Yes. Keeping your baby on or against your body most of the time in the early days supports frequent feeding, warmth, and bonding and can reduce sleepiness and separation issues.

instruction: The nurse brought my baby back all swaddled. Should I keep him wrapped or take the swaddle off?
output: Unwrap him and snuggle his bare or lightly clothed skin against yours with your blankets over both of you. He will quickly regain heat and you can keep him with you from that point on.

instruction: I keep hearing that swaddling is helpful, but is it safe for an ordinary newborn?
output: Swaddling can calm some babies with special concerns, but for ordinary newborns it is not routinely recommended. Studies suggest swaddling may be associated with lower temperature, reduced movement, and slower early weight gain, and sleeping while swaddled can be risky.

instruction: My nipples hurt and sometimes sting when I nurse. Is that normal, and what should I do?
output: Breastfeeding is not supposed to hurt. Nipples may be supersensitive at first and you might feel some stinging for a day or two from friction, which can be normal. Persistent pain or damage is not normal. Often small adjustments to positioning or letting the baby lead more will help. If pain continues, seek a knowledgeable breastfeeding helper quickly — ask friends for recommendations or contact La Leche League.

instruction: What should I watch or listen for to tell if my baby is actually swallowing milk?
output: Diaper output tells you a lot. By about three days you’ll probably notice swallowing sounds. A common swallow sound is a quiet "keh keh keh" roughly once per second; you may also see the lower jaw drop slightly with a pause and close motion that indicates a swallow.

instruction: My baby makes quick little sucks at first and then changes — what does that mean?
output: Those quick, fluttery sucks when he first latches often mean he's organizing his mouth and settling into a position to massage your breast and trigger milk flow. After that he usually moves to slower, deeper sucks when he is getting fuller mouthfuls.

instruction: People keep telling me everything looks fine but I’m terrified I'm not doing this right. Any simple advice to get through the first weeks?
output: Put the baby first — he needs to nurse and be held intensely for a couple of weeks. Keep supplies nearby where you eat, sleep, and nurse; make several places to nurse and use whatever setup helps you. Rest and accept help; practical simplicity will get you through.

instruction: If rearranging positioning doesn't stop nipple damage, who should I contact for help?
output: Find a good helper quickly: ask friends for recommendations and contact La Leche League or another experienced breastfeeding support person. Be cautious about advice that uses absolute phrases like “the right way”; experienced helpers who listen to your situation are best.

instruction: Is it dangerous to sleep with my baby if I swaddle him so he can't move?
output: Yes. A swaddled baby cannot move to protect their airway and is at higher risk if bed-sharing. While many mothers and babies sleep safely together under appropriate conditions, bed-sharing with a swaddled infant is risky and should be avoided.

instruction: How soon will I hear swallowing sounds at the breast?
output: By about three days you’ll probably start to notice swallowing. In the first couple of days the amount of colostrum varies and swallowing sounds may be intermittent, but you may hear quiet "keh" sounds or see the jaw drop-pause-close motion that indicates a swallow.

instruction: Are mothers affected when their newborn is swaddled a lot?
output: Yes. Mothers whose newborns are swaddled much of the time may experience more engorgement and lower early milk supply, and some studies note these mothers may handle babies less responsively; minimizing unnecessary swaddling can support feeding and bonding.

instruction: My baby was nursed well in the hospital bed but we can’t make it work anywhere else. Any practical tips?
output: Try multiple nursing setups at home. Some parents use a pad of blankets on the living room floor or set up different chairs and pillows as they experiment. Use whatever works — comfort and regular feeding are the priorities.

instruction: My nipples have cracked and bled. Should breastfeeding hurt like this?
output: Breastfeeding is not supposed to hurt. Cracked or bleeding nipples are not normal and need attention. Often adjustments to latch or positioning help, but if bleeding or damage persists, seek prompt help from a skilled breastfeeding helper or clinician.

instruction: My newborn doesn't seem to swallow when he first latches. Is that normal?
output: Yes. At first he may make quick, fluttery little sucks as he organizes his mouth; after he settles he will typically move to slower sucks when he gets mouthfuls of colostrum or milk.

instruction: How can I tell if breastfeeding is actually working?
output: Signs that breastfeeding is working include the baby moving from quick, organizing sucks to slower, deeper sucks when taking milk, adequate diaper output, and appropriate weight gain over the first days and weeks.

instruction: I'm terrified and overwhelmed — what should I focus on in the first days with the baby?
output: Focus on feeding and holding the baby; these needs will be intense for a couple of weeks. After the baby, take care of yourself: rest, eat, and accept help so you can heal and feed well. Other tasks can wait.

instruction: Where should I nurse the baby at home?
output: Set up several possible nursing spots and keep baby supplies centralized where you are likely to be eating, sleeping, and nursing. Use whatever location and position help you and the baby to feed comfortably.

instruction: What practical things can my partner do to help with breastfeeding?
output: Partners can feed and hydrate the mother, keep easy-to-eat foods on hand, set up comfortable nursing spaces, monitor visitors, run errands, and call for breastfeeding help if needed. Practical support and flexibility are very helpful.

instruction: I'm worried about people visiting — how should visitors be handled after the baby arrives?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure. Monitor visitors and set boundaries; if someone has overstayed or interferes with rest and feeding, politely ask them to leave or give them simple tasks to help while they visit.

instruction: I feel clumsy learning to breastfeed. Will I get better?
output: Yes. Breastfeeding often improves with practice. Expect some wobbling at first; with time, instinct, common sense, and trial and error you will become more confident and skilled.

instruction: What does 'put yourself solidly next' mean for a new mother?
